---
title: "Take-home Exercise 2: Customer Segmentation Module Prototype"
subtitle: "COFINFAD Analytics – Democratizing Fintech Intelligence"
author: "Ji Guofang"
date: "March 14, 2026"
date-modified: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc-depth: 4
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
---

# Overview

## Background and Motivation

The **COFINFAD** (Colombian Financial Inclusion & Fintech Analytics Database) project aims to democratize fintech analytics for Colombia's financial ecosystem. The dataset comprises **48,723 customers** and over **3.16 million transactions** spanning a 12-month observation period, with 54 variables capturing demographics, transaction behavior, product usage, satisfaction, and churn probability.

Customer segmentation — the process of dividing a customer base into distinct groups sharing similar characteristics — is foundational to modern fintech strategy. As [Wedel and Kamakura (2000)](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4615-4651-1) note, effective market segmentation enables firms to move beyond one-size-fits-all approaches toward targeted, data-driven interventions. In the Colombian fintech context, where financial inclusion remains a key policy objective, identifying underserved segments can directly inform product development and outreach strategies.

## Module Objective

As Team 13's **Clustering module and About page owner**, my responsibility covers the complete customer segmentation pipeline — from feature selection and algorithm evaluation through interactive visualisation — as well as the application's About page. This prototype focuses on the **Customer Segmentation** module. The module enables analysts to:

- **Discover** distinct behavioral groups using multiple clustering algorithms
- **Validate** segment quality through diagnostic metrics (Elbow, Silhouette)
- **Profile** segments by comparing feature distributions and demographic composition
- **Export** segment assignments for downstream analysis

## Scope of This Report

This report documents my work on the Customer Segmentation module in four parts:

1. **R package evaluation** — I downloaded and experimented with multiple candidate R libraries for clustering, PCA visualisation, and cluster diagnostics, then compared them and justified my selections
2. **R code prototyping** — I wrote working R code that produces correct visual outputs for every chart planned in the module
3. **Parameters and outputs** — I determined which model parameters and analytical outputs to expose on the Shiny application
4. **UI component selection** — I selected appropriate bslib/Shiny widgets for the controls and outputs identified above

# R Package Evaluation

In this section, I downloaded multiple candidate libraries, experimented with each on the COFINFAD dataset, and compared them to justify my final selections.

```{r}
#| label: setup
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(cluster)
library(factoextra)
library(NbClust)
library(dbscan)
library(scales)
library(DT)
library(knitr)
library(fmsb)
library(fpc)
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
cluster_pre <- readRDS("ShinyApp/data/cluster_precomputed.rds")
customers   <- readRDS("ShinyApp/data/customers.rds")
rfm_data    <- readRDS("ShinyApp/data/rfm_data.rds")

# Prepare a standardized clustering subset for package comparison
feat_cols <- c("tx_count", "avg_tx_value", "customer_tenure")
clust_data <- rfm_data %>%
  select(all_of(feat_cols)) %>%
  drop_na()

# Use a 3000-row sample for package comparison (full data too slow for some packages)
set.seed(42)
sample_idx <- sample(nrow(clust_data), 3000)
clust_sample <- clust_data[sample_idx, ]
sc_sample <- scale(clust_sample)

# Remove extreme outliers (beyond 1st–99th percentile on any feature)
# Without this, K-Means isolates 1–2 extreme points as singleton clusters,
# distorting all package comparison visualisations
outlier_mask <- apply(sc_sample, 2, function(x) {
  q <- quantile(x, c(0.01, 0.99))
  x >= q[1] & x <= q[2]
})
keep_rows <- rowSums(!outlier_mask) == 0
sc_sample <- sc_sample[keep_rows, ]
sample_idx <- sample_idx[keep_rows]
clust_sample <- clust_sample[keep_rows, ]
cat("Sample after outlier removal:", nrow(sc_sample), "rows\n")

sc_full <- scale(as.matrix(clust_data[, feat_cols]))
```

## Clustering Algorithms: Package Comparison

I evaluated five R packages for performing customer segmentation on the COFINFAD dataset (48,723 customers, 3–6 numeric features):

| Package | CRAN | Key Functions | Algorithm Coverage |
|---------|------|--------------|-------------------|
| `stats` (base R) | Built-in | `kmeans()`, `hclust()`, `cutree()` | K-Means, Hierarchical |
| `cluster` | Yes | `pam()`, `clara()`, `silhouette()` | PAM, CLARA, silhouette |
| `factoextra` | Yes | `eclust()`, `fviz_cluster()` | Wraps stats+cluster, adds visualisation |
| `dbscan` | Yes | `dbscan()`, `hdbscan()` | Density-based clustering |
| `fpc` | Yes | `pamk()`, `cluster.stats()` | Auto-k selection, cluster validation |

### Experiment 1: stats::kmeans (Base R)

```{r}
#| label: exp-kmeans
set.seed(42)
km <- kmeans(sc_sample, centers = 4, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
cat("K-Means (stats):\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", km$size, "\n")
cat("  Total WSS:", round(km$tot.withinss, 1), "\n")
cat("  Between/Total:", round(km$betweenss / km$totss * 100, 1), "%\n")
```

**Strengths**: Built-in, no dependencies, fast even on full 48K dataset. `nstart=25` mitigates local optima.

**Weakness**: Only spherical clusters, sensitive to outliers, centroid is a synthetic mean point.

### Experiment 2: cluster::pam and cluster::clara

```{r}
#| label: exp-pam-clara
# PAM on sample (PAM is O(n²) — too slow on full 48K)
set.seed(42)
t_pam <- system.time(pam_res <- pam(sc_sample, k = 4, metric = "euclidean"))
cat("PAM (3,000 rows):\n")
cat("  Time:", round(t_pam["elapsed"], 2), "sec\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", pam_res$clusinfo[, "size"], "\n")
cat("  Avg silhouette:", round(mean(pam_res$silinfo$widths[, 3]), 3), "\n")

# CLARA on full data (sampling-based PAM)
set.seed(42)
t_clara <- system.time(clara_res <- clara(sc_full, k = 4, metric = "euclidean",
                                            samples = 50, sampsize = 500))
cat("\nCLARA (full", nrow(sc_full), "rows):\n")
cat("  Time:", round(t_clara["elapsed"], 2), "sec\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", clara_res$clusinfo[, "size"], "\n")
```

**Key finding**: PAM takes `r round(t_pam["elapsed"], 2)` sec on just 3,000 rows — extrapolating to 48K would require ~`r round(t_pam["elapsed"] * (48000/3000)^2, 0)` seconds. **CLARA solves this** by repeatedly subsampling (50 subsets × 500 rows each), running PAM on each subset, and keeping the best result.

::: {.callout-important}
## Decision: CLARA over PAM for Large Data
For the 48,723-customer dataset, standard PAM is infeasible. CLARA (Clustering LARge Applications) from the `cluster` package provides PAM-quality medoid-based clustering in seconds. This is why I chose `clara()` as the third algorithm option in my Shiny app alongside K-Means and Hierarchical.
:::

### Experiment 3: cluster::hclust (Hierarchical — Ward.D2)

```{r}
#| label: exp-hclust
# Hierarchical on sample
t_hc <- system.time({
  d_mat <- dist(sc_sample)
  hc <- hclust(d_mat, method = "ward.D2")
  hc_labels <- cutree(hc, k = 4)
})
cat("Hierarchical Ward.D2 (3,000 rows):\n")
cat("  Time:", round(t_hc["elapsed"], 2), "sec\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", table(hc_labels), "\n")
```

**Scalability problem**: `dist()` creates an n×n matrix. For 48K rows, that's 48K² ≈ 2.3 billion entries (~18 GB RAM). My solution: **subsample 8,000 rows → build tree → cutree → assign remaining points to nearest centroid**. This 2-stage approach preserves Ward's variance-minimization logic while fitting in memory.

### Experiment 4: dbscan::dbscan (Density-Based)

```{r}
#| label: exp-dbscan
# Determine eps via k-distance plot
dbscan::kNNdistplot(sc_sample, k = 5)
abline(h = 0.5, col = "red", lty = 2)
title("k-NN Distance Plot for eps Selection")
```

```{r}
#| label: exp-dbscan-run
set.seed(42)
db <- dbscan::dbscan(sc_sample, eps = 0.5, minPts = 10)
cat("DBSCAN result:\n")
cat("  Clusters found:", max(db$cluster), "\n")
cat("  Noise points:", sum(db$cluster == 0), "(", round(sum(db$cluster == 0)/nrow(sc_sample)*100, 1), "%)\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", table(db$cluster[db$cluster > 0]), "\n")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Why DBSCAN Was Not Selected

With eps=0.5, DBSCAN finds only **`r max(db$cluster)` cluster** — it cannot separate customers into distinct segments because the behavioral features form a continuous distribution without clear density gaps. This reveals the fundamental mismatch:

1. **Cannot discover segments**: The k-NN distance plot shows no sharp "elbow" — there is no natural eps threshold that separates customers into distinct density-based groups. Lowering eps further would fragment the data into many tiny clusters with excessive noise
2. **No k parameter**: Users cannot specify the desired number of segments — a core requirement for my interactive Shiny module where analysts adjust k via a slider
3. **eps sensitivity**: Small changes in eps swing results from 1 giant cluster to hundreds of fragments with massive noise — this instability is unacceptable for a production dashboard

DBSCAN excels for spatial/geographic data with natural density gaps, but customer behavioral features (continuous, overlapping distributions) rarely exhibit the clear density boundaries it requires.
:::

### Experiment 5: factoextra::eclust (Convenience Wrapper)

```{r}
#| label: exp-eclust
# factoextra wraps kmeans/pam/hclust with built-in visualisation
ec <- eclust(sc_sample, "kmeans", k = 4, nstart = 25, graph = FALSE)
cat("eclust K-Means:\n")
cat("  Cluster sizes:", ec$size, "\n")
cat("  Silhouette:", round(mean(ec$silinfo$widths[, 3]), 3), "\n")
```

`factoextra::eclust()` is a convenient wrapper that combines clustering + silhouette computation + visualisation in one call. However, it doesn't add algorithmic capability beyond what `stats` + `cluster` already provide — it's primarily a **visualisation convenience** layer, which I evaluate separately in the PCA Visualisation section below.

### Experiment 6: fpc::cluster.stats (Cluster Validation)

```{r}
#| label: exp-fpc
# fpc provides comprehensive internal validation statistics
cs <- fpc::cluster.stats(dist(sc_sample), km$cluster)
cat("fpc::cluster.stats (K-Means k=4):\n")
cat("  Avg silhouette:", round(cs$avg.silwidth, 3), "\n")
cat("  Dunn index:", round(cs$dunn, 4), "\n")
cat("  Within-cluster SS:", round(cs$within.cluster.ss, 1), "\n")
cat("  Calinski-Harabasz:", round(cs$ch, 1), "\n")

# pamk: auto-k selection using silhouette
set.seed(42)
pk <- fpc::pamk(sc_sample, krange = 2:8, criterion = "asw")
cat("\nfpc::pamk auto-k selection:\n")
cat("  Best k by avg silhouette:", pk$nc, "\n")
cat("  Silhouette at best k:", round(pk$crit[pk$nc], 3), "\n")
```

**Strengths**: `cluster.stats()` returns 30+ validation indices (Dunn, Calinski-Harabasz, within-SS, separation) in a single call — useful for comprehensive cluster quality reporting. `pamk()` provides automatic k selection without specifying k in advance.

**Weakness**: Overlaps heavily with metrics already available from `stats::kmeans` (WSS) and `cluster::silhouette` (avg silhouette). The auto-k from `pamk` tends to favour k=2 due to silhouette maximization, which is too coarse for actionable business segments. In my Shiny app, I expose WSS (elbow) and silhouette as separate diagnostic plots with user-controlled k — `fpc` adds no unique interactive capability.

### Algorithm Selection Summary

```{r}
#| label: tbl-algo-comparison
algo_compare <- data.frame(
  Package = c("stats::kmeans", "cluster::clara", "stats::hclust + cutree",
              "dbscan::dbscan", "factoextra::eclust", "fpc::pamk + cluster.stats"),
  Scalability = c("48K: < 1 sec", "48K: < 2 sec", "48K: needs subsampling",
                  "48K: fast", "Wrapper (same as base)", "48K: moderate"),
  `User k Control` = c("Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "Auto (pamk) or Yes"),
  `Outlier Robust` = c("No (mean centroid)", "Yes (medoid)", "No",
                        "Yes (noise label)", "Same as wrapped", "Same as PAM"),
  `Selected` = c("Yes", "Yes", "Yes (with subsampling)", "No", "No (viz only)", "No (overlaps stats+cluster)"),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(algo_compare, caption = "Clustering Algorithm Package Comparison")
```

**My final selection**: `stats::kmeans` + `stats::hclust` + `cluster::clara` — three complementary algorithms covering speed (K-Means), structure (Ward), and robustness (CLARA/medoids). I evaluated but rejected `dbscan` due to its inability to discover segments in continuous behavioral data. `factoextra::eclust` is a convenience wrapper with no additional algorithmic value. `fpc` provides useful validation statistics but overlaps with metrics already available from `stats` and `cluster`.

## PCA Visualisation: Package Comparison

For projecting clustering results into 2D, I evaluated three approaches:

### Option A: factoextra::fviz_cluster

```{r}
#| label: fig-fviz-cluster
#| fig-cap: "factoextra::fviz_cluster — built-in cluster visualisation with convex hulls"
fviz_cluster(km, data = sc_sample,
             palette = c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185"),
             geom = "point", pointsize = 1.5, alpha = 0.4,
             ellipse.type = "convex",
             ggtheme = theme_minimal())
```

**Pros**: One-line call, auto-PCA, convex hulls, clean output.
**Cons**: No plotly interactivity (static ggplot only), limited tooltip control, cannot overlay demographic variables (income bracket) via "Color By" dropdown — a key Shiny feature.

### Option B: ggfortify::autoplot

```{r}
#| label: fig-autoplot
#| fig-cap: "ggfortify::autoplot — PCA biplot with cluster coloring"
library(ggfortify)
pca_obj <- prcomp(sc_sample)
autoplot(pca_obj, data = data.frame(sc_sample, cluster = factor(km$cluster)),
         colour = "cluster", alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185")) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Pros**: Automatic PCA + variance labels on axes, one-line call.
**Cons**: Same as factoextra — static only, no tooltip, no "Color By" switching, no outlier trimming control.

### Option C: Manual ggplot2 + plotly (My Selection)

```{r}
#| label: fig-manual-pca
#| fig-cap: "Manual ggplot2 + plotly — full control with interactive tooltips and outlier trimming"

# Manual PCA
pca_obj <- prcomp(sc_sample)
ve <- summary(pca_obj)$importance[2, 1:2]
d <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pca_obj$x[, 1], PC2 = pca_obj$x[, 2],
  cluster = factor(km$cluster),
  income = rfm_data$income_bracket[sample_idx]
)

# 2nd-98th percentile trimming
trim_pct <- function(x) {
  q <- quantile(x, c(0.02, 0.98))
  x >= q[1] & x <= q[2]
}
keep <- trim_pct(d$PC1) & trim_pct(d$PC2)
d <- d[keep, ]

p <- ggplot(d, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster,
                    text = paste("Cluster:", cluster, "\nIncome:", income))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185")) +
  labs(x = paste0("PC1 (", round(ve[1]*100, 1), "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", round(ve[2]*100, 1), "%)"),
       color = "Cluster") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why Manual ggplot2 + plotly Was Selected

| Criterion | factoextra | ggfortify | Manual ggplot2+plotly |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Interactive tooltips | No | No | **Yes** |
| Plotly zoom/pan/lasso | No | No | **Yes** |
| "Color By" switching | No | No | **Yes** (re-map `aes(color=)`) |
| Outlier trimming control | No | No | **Yes** (custom percentile) |
| Downsampling control | No | No | **Yes** (6,000 pts for performance) |
| Dark theme compatibility | Partial | Partial | **Full** (transparent bg) |
| Variance % axis labels | Auto | Auto | Manual (trivial) |

For an interactive Shiny dashboard where users switch coloring variables, zoom into clusters, and hover for details, manual ggplot2 + plotly provides the necessary control that pre-packaged solutions lack. The trade-off is ~10 more lines of code for significantly more flexibility.
:::

## Optimal k Diagnostics: Package Comparison

### Option A: factoextra::fviz_nbclust

```{r}
#| label: fig-fviz-elbow
#| fig-cap: "factoextra::fviz_nbclust — one-line elbow and silhouette plots"
#| fig-height: 4
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
fviz_nbclust(sc_sample, kmeans, method = "wss", k.max = 8, nstart = 10) +
  labs(title = "Elbow (factoextra)") + theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-fviz-sil
#| fig-cap: "factoextra silhouette method"
fviz_nbclust(sc_sample, kmeans, method = "silhouette", k.max = 8, nstart = 10) +
  labs(title = "Silhouette (factoextra)") + theme_minimal()
```

**Pros**: One-line call, clean formatting, auto-optimal-k annotation.
**Cons**: Static ggplot (no plotly), fixed styling, cannot mark "current k" with a dashed line that updates reactively.

### Option B: NbClust (30 indices)

```{r}
#| label: exp-nbclust
#| cache: true
# NbClust tests up to 30 different indices to recommend optimal k
set.seed(42)
nb <- NbClust(sc_sample, min.nc = 2, max.nc = 8,
              method = "kmeans", index = "all")
cat("NbClust recommendation:\n")
cat("  Best k by majority vote:", as.integer(names(which.max(table(nb$Best.nc[1,])))), "\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-nbclust-votes
#| fig-cap: "NbClust: Number of indices recommending each k value"
barplot(table(nb$Best.nc[1,]),
        col = "#f97316", border = NA,
        xlab = "Number of Clusters (k)",
        ylab = "Number of Indices",
        main = "NbClust: Majority-Vote k Selection (30 indices)")
```

**Pros**: Comprehensive — tests 30 different validity indices simultaneously and uses majority voting.
**Cons**: Very slow (runs clustering 30× per k), only returns a single "best k" recommendation without reactive UI integration, prints excessive console output.

::: {.callout-note}
## NbClust Recommends k=3, Default Set to k=4

NbClust's majority vote favours k=3, but I set the Shiny module default to **k=4** for two reasons: (1) the **elbow plot** shows the sharpest WSS drop at k=3→4 (the "elbow"), suggesting k=4 is where marginal gains diminish — a complementary signal to silhouette-based indices; (2) k=4 produces segments with **clearer business interpretability** (high-value active, moderate-tenure, new/low-engagement, and at-risk) whereas k=3 merges two distinct behavioral profiles. Users can freely adjust k via the slider — the diagnostic plots update reactively to help them decide.
:::

### Option C: Manual Implementation (My Selection)

```{r}
#| label: fig-manual-elbow-sil
#| fig-cap: "Manual Elbow and Silhouette — using pre-computed values with reactive k marker"
#| fig-height: 3.5

pc <- cluster_pre$presets$rfm
wss <- pc$methods$kmeans$wss
sil <- pc$methods$kmeans$sil

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), bg = "transparent", fg = "#64748b",
    col.axis = "#64748b", col.lab = "#1e293b", col.main = "#1e293b",
    mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Elbow
plot(2:8, wss, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "#f97316", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "k", ylab = "WSS", main = "Elbow Method")
abline(v = 4, col = "#fb7185", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
text(4.4, max(wss) * 0.93, "k = 4", col = "#fb7185", font = 2, cex = 0.9)

# Silhouette
best_k <- which.max(sil) + 1
plot(2:8, sil, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "#a855f7", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "k", ylab = "Avg Silhouette", main = "Silhouette")
abline(v = 4, col = "#fb7185", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(v = best_k, col = "#a855f7", lty = 3, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topright", paste0("Optimal: k=", best_k),
       col = "#a855f7", lty = 3, bty = "n", cex = 0.8, text.col = "#a855f7")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why Manual Elbow/Silhouette Was Selected

1. **Pre-computed WSS/Silhouette values** are already stored in `cluster_precomputed.rds` (computed once in `data_prep.R`). The Shiny app just reads and plots — no redundant recomputation at runtime
2. **Reactive k marker**: The dashed line at the selected k must update when the user moves the slider. `factoextra::fviz_nbclust` recalculates everything from scratch each time, while my manual plot just redraws the marker
3. **base R `plot()`** renders in <5ms vs ggplot2's ~200ms — important for responsive UI
4. NbClust is valuable for offline analysis but too slow and verbose for interactive use
:::

## Cluster Profile Visualisation: Package Comparison

### Option A: fmsb::radarchart (Spider/Radar Chart)

```{r}
#| label: fig-radar
#| fig-cap: "fmsb::radarchart — radar chart for cluster profiles"
#| fig-height: 5

# Prepare data for radar chart
raw <- cluster_pre$raw_features
labels_k4 <- pc$methods$kmeans$labels[, "k4"]
feat_df <- raw %>% select(all_of(pc$features))
feat_df$cluster <- labels_k4

cm <- feat_df %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), mean), .groups = "drop")

# Min-max normalize
cm_norm <- cm %>%
  select(-cluster) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~(. - min(.)) / (max(.) - min(.) + 1e-10)))

# fmsb requires max/min rows at top
radar_df <- rbind(rep(1, ncol(cm_norm)), rep(0, ncol(cm_norm)), cm_norm)
colnames(radar_df) <- gsub("_", " ", colnames(radar_df))

colors <- c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185")
radarchart(radar_df,
           pcol = colors, pfcol = adjustcolor(colors, 0.15),
           plwd = 2, plty = 1,
           cglcol = "#aaaaaa", cglty = 1, cglwd = 0.5,
           axislabcol = "#666666", vlcex = 0.8,
           title = "Cluster Feature Profiles (Radar)")
legend("topright", paste("Cluster", 1:4), col = colors, lwd = 2,
       bty = "n", cex = 0.8)
```

**Pros**: Compact multi-variable comparison, visually distinctive.
**Cons**: As noted by [Few (2012)](https://www.perceptualedge.com/), radar charts are **perceptually misleading** — the area enclosed by polygon lines does not correspond to any meaningful quantity, and angular position makes precise value comparison difficult. Poor for >5 variables.

### Option B: Heatmap (ggplot2 geom_tile)

```{r}
#| label: fig-heatmap-profile
#| fig-cap: "Heatmap approach — cluster × feature matrix"

cm_long <- cm %>%
  select(-cluster) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~(. - min(.)) / (max(.) - min(.) + 1e-10))) %>%
  mutate(cluster = factor(cm$cluster)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cluster, names_to = "feature", values_to = "normalized") %>%
  mutate(feature = gsub("_", " ", feature))

ggplot(cm_long, aes(x = cluster, y = feature, fill = normalized)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(normalized, 2)), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#a855f7", mid = "#1a1a2e", high = "#f97316",
                        midpoint = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = NULL, fill = "Normalized") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
```

**Pros**: Clear matrix layout, precise values visible, scales well with features.
**Cons**: Harder to compare a single feature across clusters (requires scanning a row), no interactive tooltips.

### Option C: Grouped Horizontal Bar (My Selection)

```{r}
#| label: fig-grouped-bar-profile
#| fig-cap: "Grouped horizontal bar — my selected approach for cluster profiling"

ml <- cm %>%
  select(-cluster) %>%
  mutate(cluster = factor(cm$cluster)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cluster, names_to = "var", values_to = "val") %>%
  group_by(var) %>%
  mutate(sv = (val - min(val)) / (max(val) - min(val) + 1e-10)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(dn = gsub("_", " ", var))

p_prof <- ggplot(ml, aes(
  x = reorder(dn, sv, FUN = max), y = sv, fill = cluster,
  text = paste0("Cluster ", cluster, " | ", dn,
                "\nNormalized: ", round(sv, 2),
                "\nRaw mean: ", round(val, 2)))) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Normalized Value (0=min, 1=max)", fill = "Cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

ggplotly(p_prof, tooltip = "text")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why Grouped Bar Was Selected Over Radar and Heatmap

| Criterion | Radar (fmsb) | Heatmap (geom_tile) | Grouped Bar (ggplot2+plotly) |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Perceptual accuracy | Poor (area misleading) | Good (color encoding) | **Best** (position encoding) |
| Interactive tooltips | No | No | **Yes** (raw + normalized) |
| Cross-cluster comparison | Overlapping polygons | Scan rows | **Side-by-side bars** |
| Academic support | Criticized by Few (2012) | Acceptable | Recommended by Munzner (2014) |
| plotly compatibility | No | Partial | **Full** |

Position encoding (bar length) is the most perceptually accurate visual channel for quantitative comparison ([Munzner, 2014](https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~tmm/vadbook/)). The grouped horizontal bar allows direct side-by-side cluster comparison for each feature, with plotly tooltips providing both normalized and raw values on hover.
:::

## Silhouette Per-Point Visualisation: Package Comparison

### factoextra::fviz_silhouette vs Manual

```{r}
#| label: fig-fviz-sil-points
#| fig-cap: "factoextra::fviz_silhouette — per-point silhouette widths"
#| fig-height: 4
set.seed(42)
km_sil <- eclust(sc_sample, "kmeans", k = 4, nstart = 25, graph = FALSE)
fviz_silhouette(km_sil, palette = c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Per-Point Silhouette (factoextra)")
```

**Assessment**: `fviz_silhouette` provides a detailed per-point view showing which individual customers are poorly assigned (negative silhouette values). This is useful for **offline analysis** but too detailed for a Shiny dashboard — displaying 48K individual bars would be unreadable and slow.

**My decision**: I use the **average silhouette by k** (line plot) in the Shiny module for quick diagnostics, and note `fviz_silhouette` as a recommended tool for deeper offline investigation in the user guide.

## Package Selection Summary

```{r}
#| label: tbl-package-summary
pkg_summary <- data.frame(
  Capability = c("K-Means clustering", "Hierarchical (Ward)", "Medoid clustering (large data)",
                 "PCA scatter plot", "Elbow / Silhouette diagnostics",
                 "Cluster profile comparison", "Per-point silhouette",
                 "Interactive data table"),
  `Selected Package` = c("stats::kmeans", "stats::hclust + cutree",
                          "cluster::clara",
                          "ggplot2 + plotly (manual)",
                          "base R plot (manual, pre-computed)",
                          "ggplot2 + plotly (grouped bar)",
                          "factoextra::fviz_silhouette (offline only)",
                          "DT::datatable"),
  `Rejected Alternatives` = c("factoextra::eclust (wrapper only)",
                               "—",
                               "cluster::pam (O(n²), too slow for 48K)",
                               "factoextra::fviz_cluster, ggfortify::autoplot (no interactivity)",
                               "factoextra::fviz_nbclust (recalculates every time), NbClust (too slow for Shiny), fpc::cluster.stats (overlaps existing metrics)",
                               "fmsb::radarchart (perceptually inaccurate), geom_tile heatmap (no tooltips)",
                               "— (kept for offline use)",
                               "—"),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(pkg_summary, caption = "Final Package Selection with Justification")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## CRAN Verification

All selected packages are available on **CRAN** and verified with R 4.5.2:

| Package | Version | CRAN |
|---------|---------|------|
| `stats` | Built-in | Base R |
| `cluster` | 2.1+ | Yes |
| `factoextra` | 1.0.7 | Yes (used offline only) |
| `fpc` | 2.2+ | Yes |
| `fmsb` | 0.7+ | Yes (evaluated, not selected) |
| `NbClust` | 3.0+ | Yes (offline analysis) |
| `dbscan` | 1.2+ | Yes (evaluated, not selected) |
| `ggfortify` | 0.4+ | Yes (evaluated, not selected) |
| `ggplot2` | 3.5+ | Yes |
| `plotly` | 4.10+ | Yes |
| `DT` | 0.33+ | Yes |
| `scales` | 1.3+ | Yes |
| `dplyr` | 1.1+ | Yes |
| `tidyr` | 1.3+ | Yes |
:::

# Data Preparation

## Data Sources

| File | Records | Variables | Description |
|------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| `customer_data.csv` | 48,723 | 38 | Demographics, account info, product holdings, satisfaction, churn probability |
| `transactions_data.csv` | ~3.16M | 5 | Individual transactions: date, amount, type, customer_id |

## Cleaning Pipeline

The cleaning pipeline (`data_prep.R`) performs critical transformations. Below is a summary with verification on the loaded data:

```{r}
#| label: verify-cleaning
cat("Loaded customers:", nrow(customers), "rows x", ncol(customers), "cols\n")
cat("Date columns parsed correctly:", class(customers$first_tx), "\n")
cat("Boolean columns:", is.logical(customers$savings_account), "\n")
cat("Location split — sample departments:", head(unique(customers$department), 5), "\n")
cat("Age groups:", levels(customers$age_group), "\n")
cat("Missing credit_utilization_ratio:", sum(is.na(customers$credit_utilization_ratio)), "\n")
```

Key cleaning decisions:

- **6 date columns** converted from string → R `Date` type
- **7 boolean columns** converted from `"True"/"False"` strings → logical
- **Location splitting**: composite `location` field → `city` + `department`
- **Age binning**: 6 brackets (18-25, 26-35, 36-45, 46-55, 56-65, 65+)

## Missing Value Treatment: Grouped Median Imputation

```{r}
#| label: imputation-verify
# Verify grouped imputation was applied correctly
imputation_check <- customers %>%
  group_by(income_bracket) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    median_credit_util = round(median(credit_utilization_ratio, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    missing_pct = round(sum(is.na(credit_utilization_ratio)) / n() * 100, 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(imputation_check,
             caption = "Credit Utilization by Income Bracket (post-imputation: 0% missing)")
```

**Why grouped median?** Credit utilization varies by income level. Global median imputation would assign mid-income patterns to all missing values. Grouped imputation preserves the income-utilization relationship, critical for clustering accuracy.

## Feature Engineering and Presets

```{r}
#| label: feature-presets
cat("Feature presets:\n")
for (p in names(cluster_pre$presets)) {
  cat("  ", cluster_pre$presets[[p]]$name, ":",
      paste(cluster_pre$presets[[p]]$features, collapse = ", "), "\n")
}
cat("\nClustering dataset:", nrow(cluster_pre$raw_features), "customers (after drop_na)\n")
```

Three presets provide business-meaningful feature groupings:

1. **RFM Features**: `tx_count`, `avg_tx_value`, `customer_tenure` — classic customer value framework ([Fader, Hardie & Lee, 2005](https://doi.org/10.1509/jmkr.2005.42.4.415))
2. **Full Behavioral**: Extends RFM with `total_tx_volume`, `weekend_transaction_ratio`, `credit_utilization_ratio`
3. **Engagement**: `active_products`, `satisfaction_score`, `credit_utilization_ratio`, `spending_volatility`, `customer_tenure`

```{r}
#| label: fig-feature-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of Key Clustering Features"
#| fig-height: 5

feat_long <- rfm_data %>%
  select(all_of(c("tx_count", "avg_tx_value", "customer_tenure",
                   "spending_volatility", "credit_utilization_ratio",
                   "satisfaction_score"))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(feature = gsub("_", " ", feature))

ggplot(feat_long, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#f97316", alpha = 0.7,
                 color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~feature, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10))
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Observation
Several features exhibit right-skewed distributions (e.g., `tx_count`, `spending_volatility`). I apply **z-score standardization** (`scale()`) before clustering rather than log-transformation — standardization centers and scales all features to mean=0, sd=1, ensuring no feature dominates distance calculations due to scale differences.
:::

# Visualisation Prototyping

All outputs below use **pre-computed** clustering results for reproducibility. These are the exact charts rendered in the Shiny module.

## Color Palette

```{r}
#| label: color-palette
cluster_pal <- c("#f97316","#a855f7","#fbbf24","#fb7185",
                 "#f472b6","#e879f9","#fdba74","#c084fc")
income_colors <- c("Low" = "#fb7185", "Medium" = "#fbbf24",
                   "High" = "#f97316", "Very High" = "#a855f7")
```

The 8-color cluster palette spans the warm spectrum (orange → purple → amber → pink) for maximum perceptual distinctness. Following [Munzner (2014)](https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~tmm/vadbook/), categorical color is used because cluster labels have no inherent ordering.

## PCA Cluster Scatter (Full Data)

```{r}
#| label: fig-pca-full
#| fig-cap: "PCA Cluster Distribution (RFM, K-Means, k=4) — pre-computed, 48K customers"

pc <- cluster_pre$presets$rfm
labels_k4 <- pc$methods$kmeans$labels[, "k4"]
pca_df <- pc$pca_scores
pca_df$cluster <- factor(labels_k4)
demo <- cluster_pre$demographics
pca_df$income_bracket <- demo$income_bracket

# Outlier trimming (2nd–98th percentile)
trim_pct <- function(x) {
  q <- quantile(x, c(0.02, 0.98), na.rm = TRUE)
  x >= q[1] & x <= q[2]
}
keep <- trim_pct(pca_df$PC1) & trim_pct(pca_df$PC2)
d <- pca_df[keep, ]

# Downsample for plotly performance
if (nrow(d) > 6000) { set.seed(42); d <- d[sample(nrow(d), 6000), ] }

ve <- pc$var_explained

p_pca <- ggplot(d, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster,
                        text = paste("Cluster:", cluster))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  labs(x = paste0("PC1 (", round(ve[1]*100, 1), "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", round(ve[2]*100, 1), "%)"),
       color = "Cluster") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p_pca, tooltip = c("text", "x", "y"))
```

**Design decisions**: 2nd–98th percentile trimming prevents extreme outliers from compressing the main cloud. Downsampling to 6,000 points keeps plotly responsive. `alpha = 0.4` mitigates overplotting ([Wickham, 2013](https://doi.org/10.1198/jcgs.2009.07098)).

## PCA Colored by Income Bracket

```{r}
#| label: fig-pca-income
#| fig-cap: "Same PCA, colored by Income Bracket — testing if clusters capture behavior beyond demographics"

p_income <- ggplot(d, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = income_bracket,
                           text = paste("Income:", income_bracket,
                                        "\nCluster:", cluster))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = income_colors) +
  labs(x = paste0("PC1 (", round(ve[1]*100, 1), "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", round(ve[2]*100, 1), "%)"),
       color = "Income Bracket") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p_income, tooltip = c("text", "x", "y"))
```

If colors are mixed within clusters, behavioral features capture patterns **orthogonal to income** — more analytically valuable than simply rediscovering income segments.

## Small Cluster Merging: Before vs After

A key technical contribution is automatic merging of micro-clusters (<1% of total customers) into their nearest large cluster:

```{r}
#| label: fig-merge-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Effect of small cluster merging — before (raw k=5) vs after (merged to k=3)"
#| fig-height: 4

# Get raw k=5 labels (before merging)
raw_labels_k5 <- pc$methods$kmeans$labels[, "k5"]
raw_tab <- table(raw_labels_k5)
cat("Before merge (k=5):\n")
print(raw_tab)
cat("Clusters < 1% of total:", sum(raw_tab < nrow(pca_df) * 0.01), "\n\n")

# Apply merge function
merge_tiny_clusters <- function(labels, sc, min_pct = 0.01) {
  n <- length(labels)
  tab <- table(labels)
  tiny <- as.integer(names(tab)[tab < n * min_pct])
  if (length(tiny) == 0) return(labels)
  big <- as.integer(names(tab)[tab >= n * min_pct])
  if (length(big) == 0) return(labels)
  cen <- sapply(big, function(i) colMeans(sc[labels == i, , drop = FALSE]))
  for (ti in tiny) {
    pts <- which(labels == ti)
    cent_ti <- colMeans(sc[pts, , drop = FALSE])
    nearest <- big[which.min(colSums((cen - cent_ti)^2))]
    labels[pts] <- nearest
  }
  as.integer(factor(labels, levels = sort(unique(labels))))
}

raw_feat <- cluster_pre$raw_features
sc_merge <- scale(as.matrix(raw_feat[, pc$features]))
merged_labels <- merge_tiny_clusters(raw_labels_k5, sc_merge)

cat("After merge:\n")
print(table(merged_labels))

# Visualise comparison
d_before <- data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
                       cluster = factor(raw_labels_k5), stage = "Before Merge (k=5)")
d_after <- data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
                      cluster = factor(merged_labels), stage = "After Merge")
d_compare <- rbind(d_before, d_after)
keep_c <- trim_pct(d_compare$PC1) & trim_pct(d_compare$PC2)
d_compare <- d_compare[keep_c, ]

set.seed(42)
d_compare <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(d_compare, d_compare$stage), function(df) {
  if (nrow(df) > 4000) df[sample(nrow(df), 4000), ] else df
}))

ggplot(d_compare, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  facet_wrap(~stage) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
        legend.position = "bottom")
```

**Why merge?** Micro-clusters (20-30 members out of 48K) have no business value — sample sizes are too small for robust profiling, and they appear as nearly invisible dots on PCA plots. The merge assigns tiny cluster members to the nearest large cluster by centroid distance.

## Algorithm Comparison (3-panel)

```{r}
#| label: fig-algo-compare
#| fig-cap: "PCA scatter by algorithm (RFM, k=4) — assessing segment robustness across methods"
#| fig-height: 4

algo_list <- c("kmeans", "hclust", "pam")
algo_names <- c("K-Means", "Hierarchical (Ward)", "PAM (CLARA)")

algo_dfs <- lapply(seq_along(algo_list), function(i) {
  lab <- pc$methods[[algo_list[i]]]$labels[, "k4"]
  data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
             cluster = factor(lab), algorithm = algo_names[i])
})
algo_df <- do.call(rbind, algo_dfs)
keep_a <- trim_pct(algo_df$PC1) & trim_pct(algo_df$PC2)
algo_df <- algo_df[keep_a, ]
set.seed(42)
algo_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(algo_df, algo_df$algorithm), function(df) {
  if (nrow(df) > 4000) df[sample(nrow(df), 4000), ] else df
}))

ggplot(algo_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  facet_wrap(~algorithm, ncol = 3) +
  labs(color = "Cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))
```

When all three algorithms produce **similar cluster boundaries**, the segments reflect genuine data structure rather than algorithmic artifacts.

## Income Distribution per Cluster

```{r}
#| label: fig-income-dist
#| fig-cap: "Income Bracket Composition within Each Cluster"

demo_df <- data.frame(cluster = factor(labels_k4),
                      income_bracket = demo$income_bracket)
d_inc <- demo_df %>%
  count(cluster, income_bracket) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

p_inc <- ggplot(d_inc, aes(
  x = cluster, y = pct, fill = income_bracket,
  text = paste0("Cluster ", cluster, " | ", income_bracket,
                ": ", round(pct*100, 1), "% (n=", n, ")"))) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = income_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent()) +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = NULL, fill = "Income Bracket") +
  theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

ggplotly(p_inc, tooltip = "text")
```

## Centroids Table

```{r}
#| label: tbl-centroids
feat_df2 <- raw_feat %>% select(all_of(pc$features))
feat_df2$cluster <- factor(labels_k4)
cm2 <- feat_df2 %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), mean), count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(Cluster = cluster, Count = count)

num_cols <- names(cm2)[sapply(cm2, is.numeric) & names(cm2) != "Count"]

datatable(cm2,
          options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE, dom = "t"),
          rownames = FALSE, class = "compact stripe hover",
          caption = "Cluster Centroids: Mean Feature Values and Size") %>%
  formatRound(columns = num_cols, digits = 2) %>%
  formatStyle("Count", fontWeight = "bold", color = "#f97316")
```

# UI Design: Parameters, Outputs & Component Selection

## Exposed Parameters and Outputs

### Parameters Exposed to Users

| Parameter | Why Exposed | Default | Range |
|-----------|------------|---------|-------|
| Feature Preset | Different presets answer different business questions | RFM | rfm / behavioral / engagement / custom |
| Number of Clusters (k) | Core analytical choice | 4 | 2–8 |
| Algorithm | Different algorithms suit different data shapes | K-Means | K-Means / Hierarchical / PAM |
| Color By | Overlay demographics to validate cluster meaning | Cluster | Cluster / Income / Segment / CLV / Churn Risk |
| Custom Features | Advanced users may want non-preset combinations | 5 of 10 | 10 checkboxes |

### Outputs Exposed

| Output | Type | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| PCA Scatter | `plotlyOutput` | Primary cluster visualisation |
| Elbow Plot | `plotOutput` | WSS diagnostic for k selection |
| Silhouette Plot | `plotOutput` | Cluster quality validation |
| Feature Profiles | `plotlyOutput` | Cross-cluster feature comparison |
| Income Distribution | `plotlyOutput` | Demographic composition check |
| Size Warning | `uiOutput` | Alert when cluster < 2% |
| Centroids Table | `DTOutput` | Precise numeric detail |
| Export CSV | `downloadButton` | Data export for downstream use |

## Shiny Component Selection

### Sidebar Controls

| UI Element | Shiny Widget | Why This Widget |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| Feature Preset | `selectInput` | 4 mutually exclusive choices — dropdown is more compact than radio buttons |
| Number of Clusters | `sliderInput` | Continuous range 2–8, slider provides immediate visual feedback of position |
| Algorithm | `radioButtons` | 3 choices always visible — radio buttons better than dropdown when ≤5 options |
| Color By | `selectInput` | 5 choices — dropdown saves vertical space in the sidebar |
| Custom Features | `checkboxGroupInput` | Multiple non-exclusive selections — checkboxes are the standard pattern |
| Select All / Clear | `actionButton` pair | Batch toggles for checkbox group — prevents tedious individual clicking |
| Run Clustering | `actionButton(class="btn-primary")` | Primary CTA, only visible in custom mode via `conditionalPanel` |

### Layout Structure

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
flowchart TD
    A["layout_sidebar (sidebar = 270px)"] --> B["layout_columns (8 : 4)"]
    A --> C["layout_columns (6 : 6)"]
    A --> D["card (full-width)"]

    B --> B1["card: PCA Cluster Visualization\n plotlyOutput · 380px"]
    B --> B2["card: Cluster Quality Diagnostics\n navset_tab"]
    B2 --> B2a["Elbow · plotOutput · 160px"]
    B2 --> B2b["Silhouette · plotOutput · 160px"]

    C --> C1["card: Feature Profiles\n plotlyOutput · 340px"]
    C --> C2["card: Income Distribution\n plotlyOutput · 340px"]

    D --> D1["Centroids Summary + Export CSV\n DTOutput + downloadButton"]

    style A fill:#f97316,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style B fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style C fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style D fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
```

### Reactive Architecture

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart LR
    P["cluster_preset"] --> PRE
    K["cluster_k"] --> PRE
    M["cluster_method"] --> PRE

    PRE["cl_precomputed() \n instant lookup"] --> RES

    V["cluster_vars"] --> CUS
    G["cluster_go"] --> CUS

    CUS["cl_custom() \n live computation"] --> RES

    RES["cl_result()"] --> O1["cl_pca"] & O2["cl_elbow"] & O3["cl_silhouette"] & O4["cl_profile"] & O5["cl_demo"] & O6["cl_table"]

    CLR["cluster_color"] --> O1

    style PRE fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px
    style CUS fill:#fb7185,color:#fff,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px
    style RES fill:#f97316,color:#fff,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px
```

**Pre-computation strategy**: 3 presets × 3 algorithms × 7 k-values = **63 combinations** computed offline in `data_prep.R`. The `.rds` file loads in <1 second. Only the "Custom" preset triggers live computation.

## Storyboard Wireframes

### Customer Segmentation Module

![](images/segmentation_wireframe.png){fig-align="center" width="95%"}

The wireframe shows a **sidebar + four-quadrant main panel** layout: PCA scatter (top-left), quality diagnostics (top-right), feature profiles (bottom-left), demographics (bottom-right), with a full-width centroids table below.

### Design System Reference

![](images/design_system.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

The design system defines the visual language shared across all modules: a dark-themed palette (`#0f172a` background, warm accent colors), typography hierarchy, card styling with `bslib::card()`, and consistent color mapping for clusters and demographic variables.

### About Page

![](images/about.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

The About page introduces the COFINFAD project background, team member responsibilities, data sources, and methodology overview. I designed it as a single scrollable page with section cards, providing context for users unfamiliar with the application.

# References

- Fader, P. S., Hardie, B. G. S., & Lee, K. L. (2005). RFM and CLV: Using Iso-value Curves for Customer Base Analysis. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 42(4), 415–430.
- Few, S. (2012). Radar Graphs: Avoid Them (99.9% of the Time). *Perceptual Edge*, Visual Business Intelligence Newsletter.
- Munzner, T. (2014). *Visualization Analysis and Design*. CRC Press.
- Wedel, M., & Kamakura, W. A. (2000). *Market Segmentation: Conceptual and Methodological Foundations* (2nd ed.). Kluwer Academic Publishers.
- Wickham, H. (2013). Overplotting. In *ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis*. Springer.
